ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tom yum soup entry; no seafood-specific red variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers seafood soup broadly; no specific tom yum seafood red variant code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for tom yum seafood soup. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of seafood tom yum soup (red) is approximately 450 g, providing about 302 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 14.1 g fat, and 29.9 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of approximately 3285 mg per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Tom Yum Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained as indicative values and flagged with reduced confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood tom yum soup (red). GI estimated at 48 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood soup with coconut milk and glass noodles. The carbohydrate contributors are coconut milk (negligible GI impact), glass noodles/starch (GI ~26–39 for mung bean noodles per University of Sydney GI Database), and tomato/vegetable components (low GI). Weighted average across components yields an estimated dish GI of approximately 45–52; midpoint 48 used. GL per 450 g serving = (48 × 29.9) / 100 = 14.4 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 730 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; a single 450 g bowl delivers approximately 3285 mg sodium, well above the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request significantly reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste, and avoid drinking the broth — consume only the seafood and vegetables to minimise sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 6.64 g/100 g with sugar 4.25 g/100 g; a 450 g bowl provides approximately 29.9 g carbohydrate; estimated GI of 48 (low) limits acute glycaemic impact, but coconut milk saturated fat and glass noodle starch moderately elevate metabolic load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in moderation; request the soup without glass noodles to reduce carbohydrate load, choose the evaporated milk variant over coconut milk to lower saturated fat, and monitor portion size to keep total carbohydrate per meal within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 730 mg/100 g (3285 mg per bowl) poses significant cardiovascular risk; saturated fat 2.0 g/100 g from coconut milk and cholesterol 36 mg/100 g are secondary concerns; seafood omega-3 content is a partial mitigating factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content; if consumed, request reduced seasoning, avoid the broth, and choose the evaporated milk variant to lower saturated fat; the seafood components provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (lemongrass, galangal, polyphenols), seafood omega-3, and vitamin C from lime and coriander are beneficial; however, high sodium (730 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling, and nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory components of this soup; request reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste to lower sodium, monitor individual tolerance to nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli padi), and prefer the evaporated milk variant to keep saturated fat below the RA threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Tom Yum Soup (Red) is a Thai-style spicy and sour soup made with a rich red tom yum paste base, coconut milk or evaporated milk broth, and a combination of mixed seafood including prawns, squid, and fish fillet. The soup is characterised by its bold, complex flavour profile — simultaneously hot from chilli padi, sour from lime juice, and aromatic from lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves. Mushrooms, tomato, and coriander are common additions. The red variant uses red chilli-based tom yum paste, giving the broth a deeper colour and richer heat compared to the clear (white) variant. Per 100 g, the dish provides 67 kcal, 2.95 g protein, 3.14 g fat, and 6.64 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 730 mg. A standard 450 g bowl delivers approximately 302 kcal and 3285 mg sodium — making sodium the primary clinical concern. The estimated glycemic index is 48 (low), driven by the low-GI glass noodles and vegetable components. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its very high sodium load from fish sauce and tom yum paste. It is borderline for Type 2 Diabetes management due to moderate carbohydrate content and coconut milk saturated fat. The anti-inflammatory aromatics and seafood omega-3 content make it conditionally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients who can tolerate nightshades. This dish is widely consumed in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants, and is popular as a warming, flavourful main-meal soup.
common_user_queries: Is seafood tom yum soup healthy?
How much sodium is in tom yum soup?
Can diabetics eat tom yum soup?
Is tom yum soup good for high blood pressure?
What is the difference between red and white tom yum soup?
How many calories in a bowl of seafood tom yum soup?
Can I eat tom yum soup if I have heart disease?
What seafood is in tom yum soup?
Is tom yum soup gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Secondary micronutrients retained from input at reduced confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology; direct measurement not available.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific seafood tom yum soup (red) preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 16 mg, iron 0.5 mg, cholesterol 36 mg, saturated fat 2.0 g, trans fat 0.02 g, vitamin C 5.0 mg) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source (grabfood); these values are plausible for this dish type and have been retained as indicative estimates at reduced confidence (0.7) pending verification against an approved primary source. The input record contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled '1 bowl' with different weights (450 g and 500 g); 450 g has been adopted as the default serving size as the more conservative estimate. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input is incorrect for a seafood dish and has been removed. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is recommended for clinical applications. Halal status assumed based on typical hawker preparation; kosher status not applicable. Trans fat 0.02 g/100 g is a trace amount from coconut milk processing; this is a confirmed near-zero value from the input source.
